one_line_takeaway: Swapping the fixed 'affirmative-response' target for a REINFORCE objective that directly optimizes the model's own probability of producing a judged-harmful answer roughly doubles jailbreak success for GCG/PGD and breaks a circuit-breaker defense (2%->50% ASR), showing static objectives badly overestimate LLM robustness.
key_finding: Across five aligned models on 200 HarmBench standard behaviors, the REINFORCE objective consistently and substantially raises attack success rate (ASR@512, HarmBench Llama-2-13B judge) over the affirmative objective while changing nothing else in the attacks. For GCG on Llama 3 8B, ASR@512 more than doubles from 0.35 to 0.73; on Llama 2 7B it rises 0.32->0.56; Gemma 1.1 2B 0.57->0.88, Gemma 1.1 7B 0.63->0.87, Vicuna 1.5 7B 0.86->0.95. Most strikingly, against the state-of-the-art circuit-breaker defense (Zou et al., 2024) on Llama 3 8B, affirmative GCG is virtually defeated (ASR@512 = 0.02) whereas REINFORCE-GCG reaches 0.23, rising to 0.50 when seeded with harmful generations previously found on the undefended base model (y_history). This is presented as evidence that non-adaptive/inconsistent objectives grossly overestimate robustness. An ablation on Llama 3 8B shows the gains come chiefly from adding y_harmful (0.34->0.56 ASR@512) and then y_greedy (->0.73); adding y_random alone to the affirmative seed did not help and slightly hurt.
core_contribution: The paper reformulates optimization-based LLM jailbreak attacks by replacing the standard 'affirmative-response' objective (maximizing the likelihood of a fixed, human-written harmful prefix like 'Sure, here's how...') with an adaptive, distributional, and semantic objective derived via the REINFORCE policy-gradient formalism. Instead of maximizing P(y_affirmative | prompt), the attack directly maximizes the expected harmfulness reward E[Reward(y)] over the model's own distribution of sampled responses, i.e. it approximately optimizes P(Harmful | prompt). The authors argue the affirmative objective is 'inconsistent' (higher affirmative likelihood does not imply a more harmful completion) and non-adaptive (the target prefix is not tailored to the attacked model), which causes robustness to be overestimated. Their two concrete artifacts are REINFORCE-GCG and REINFORCE-PGD: drop-in modifications of the existing GCG (Zou et al., 2023) and PGD (Geisler et al., 2024) gradient-based attacks that keep all other attack machinery unchanged and only swap the loss/objective. Note: the RL framing and the 'consistency' argument (from Tramer et al., 2020) are motivated by cited prior work; the paper's OWN novelty is the REINFORCE estimator for the prompt-optimization setting plus its integration into GCG/PGD.
threat_model: White-box, gradient-based, test-time (evasion) jailbreak attack on aligned LLMs. The attacker has full access to model weights/gradients (needed for GCG's coordinate-gradient mutation and PGD's relaxed one-hot optimization), keeps model parameters fixed, and only perturbs the input by appending/prepending adversarial tokens (a 20-token suffix for GCG, 25-token prefix+suffix for PGD). The attacker also has query access to an LLM-as-a-judge (HarmBench's Llama 2 13B / Mistral 7B judges) which supplies the harmfulness reward signal. Goal: elicit a genuinely harmful completion to an inappropriate request, not merely an affirmative-sounding opening. The same objective is presented as generalizable to auditing other properties (factuality, unlearning) by swapping the reward, but only jailbreaking is evaluated.
limitations: 1) The objective is only as good as its LLM-as-a-judge reward; the authors acknowledge judge imperfections propagate into the attack (though they claim no systematic issues in sampled examples). 2) The HarmBench reward behaves almost binary (near 0 or near 1), giving little gradient guidance, which motivated the prefix-reward workaround. 3) Evaluation uses only the greedy generation as the harmfulness estimate, which is unstable in the input and does not imply random generations are harmful; more sophisticated decoding could change alignment behavior. 4) Hard compute constraints (80GB A100/H100): runtime/ablation experiments subsample to 50 of 200 behaviors, PGD batch size reduced to 17 (from 30-50), and PGD uses the weaker Mistral 7B judge during optimization for batching. 5) Base-attack hyperparameters were NOT tuned and no model-specific templates were used, so absolute numbers are conservative. 6) On Llama 2 7B, PGD gains were only marginal (0.17->0.22), the one near-null result. 7) Strong circuit-breaker results (0.50) depend on privileged y_history seeds obtained from the undefended model, and are reported only on the subset of prompts already breakable on the base model.

datasets_used: HarmBench standard behaviors (all 200 default behaviors; excludes copyright and contextual behaviors)
50-behavior subsample of HarmBench (for runtime comparisons and ablations, due to compute budget)
quantitative_results: GCG ASR@512 (affirmative -> REINFORCE): Gemma 1.1 2B 0.57->0.88; Gemma 1.1 7B 0.63->0.87; Llama 2 7B 0.32->0.56; Llama 3 8B 0.35->0.73; Vicuna 1.5 7B 0.86->0.95
PGD ASR@512 (affirmative -> REINFORCE): Gemma 1.1 2B 0.56->0.82; Gemma 1.1 7B 0.54->0.84; Llama 2 7B 0.17->0.22 (marginal); Llama 3 8B 0.57->0.69; Vicuna 1.5 7B 0.87->0.94

Llama 3 8B circuit-breaker defense, REINFORCE-GCG ASR@512: affirmative 0.02 -> REINFORCE (y_affirmative seed) 0.23 -> REINFORCE (y_history seed) 0.50* (on subset breakable on base model); ASR@128 correspondingly 0.01 -> 0.21 -> 0.46*
Ablation on Llama 3 8B GCG (ASR@128 / ASR@512): affirmative seed only 0.29/0.35; +y_random 0.22/0.36; +extend y_seed 0.24/0.34; +y_harmful 0.42/0.56; +y_greedy (full) 0.66/0.73
AdvPrefix comparison on Llama 3 8B + circuit breaker: affirmative-with-AdvPrefix ASR@512 0.14 vs REINFORCE 0.50

Attack config: GCG search width S=512, E=500 iterations, 20-token suffix init; PGD E=5000 iterations, lr alpha=0.11, 40% entropy projection, 25-token prefix+suffix init, batch of 17 prompts; generations truncated to 128 tokens during attack, evaluated at 512
baselines_compared: GCG with affirmative-response objective (Zou et al., 2023)
PGD with affirmative-response objective (Geisler et al., 2024)
AdvPrefix targets (Zhu et al., 2024) with the affirmative objective
Ablated search/selection variants: random mutation, affirmative-gradient mutation, affirmative-response selection vs REINFORCE selection (Fig. 4)
